should the abstract "return a new object" algorithm never return null?
getContext just doesn't seem to describe what happens on error--it seems like the "primary context" should also not be set on error
(webgl returns null on error, which is why I care--it doesn't seem like that's valid)
the problem is for webgl context creation should be async
since it's heavyweight and can fail
2d context creation is assumed to be pretty lightweight and won't reasonably fail
they do have "context lost"/"context restored" events, but they're used later
i'm trying to convince them to do something saner, but that's hard to do when I can't tell what getContext actually wants them to do :)
IMO: getContext should propagate exceptions from context creation, clear the "primary context" on exception if it was just set, and prohibit null as a return value entirely
what would you return in the case of a context initialization failure?
so you'd have to block the call until you knew the context was ready
but I can't recommend that to webgl when getContext itself doesn't support it
it already has to do that
since webgl returns null if the context can't be created; now it would just throw an exception instead
right. which is unfortunate for webgl but orthogonal to your concern
from talking with them this isn't totally set in stone, which is why I'm trying to nudge this towards something better before it is
(which is why I'm nagging hixie on IRC :)

I think the issues with copyright depend largely on the use cases for extracting and processing the data, and the balance between the cost of false positives and the value of true positives - without knowing that, it seems unreasonable to argue that class="copyright" is either good or bad. I would imagine the main concern is that it violates the ‚Äúcontract‚Äù made by previous specifications that class='‚Ä¶' imparts no particular meaning above providing an identification string to hang, e.g., CSS off.
@role (in the XHTML2 WD) will have similar issues if any future revisions to the spec want to add values in the global namespace
HTML4 says it's for "For general purpose processing by user agents" in addition to being a style hook, which I'd interpret as saying it can be used for whatever arbitrary things the UA wants
@profile directly references it, does it not?
(though it'd be valid to ignore what HTML4 says and only consider the implied agreement between real UAs and authors, that class is just used for styles)
That may be a point for disagreement then. Worth exploring I think.
FF3's microformat support would also be a non-styling use of class
and that won't use @profile, as too many documents with ¬µf don't use the @profile

I have a catalyst 4507 switch that i just configured. Currently connected to a another switch via fiber. Everything is working ok. All ports are assigned to vlan1 after setting the default gateway I was able to use the ports. Now I want to be able to create vlans on the switch. My goal is to create 3 vlans and move ports into them. However when doing so I loose the ability of communicating with ports when outside of the vlan.

traffic must be routed between VLANs.  your switch can do it, but then it becomes a router.  to accomplish this, create a VLAN interface for each vlan ('interface Vlan 2') and assign it an IP address.  then use this address as the default gateway for hosts on that VLAN.
Catfive: after assining the vlan interface to an ip do I have to do anything with ip routing?
you can configure 'no shutdown' on the VLAN interface and 'ip routing' globally, just to make sure things are enabled.
Catfive: so it is necessary to use the ip rotuing option globally
